included.label: Included cases
included.description: Normalized AI incident records selected for the main public dataset.

records.error_title: Air Canada chatbot misstated bereavement-fare refund eligibility
records.error_description: Air Canada's chatbot told a passenger he could request a bereavement discount retroactively within 90 days after travel. The answer conflicted with Air Canada's bereavement-fare terms, which required approval before travel. The tribunal treated the chatbot answer as part of Air Canada's customer-facing service rather than a separate legal actor.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Air Canada bereavement-fare policy
records.mitigation_gap: No effective guardrail prevented the chatbot from presenting a refund rule inconsistent with the canonical policy, and no escalation path corrected the answer before purchase.
records.reliance_or_harm: Passenger relied on the chatbot answer, bought tickets, and obtained a partial damages award.
records.public_matter_name: Moffatt v. Air Canada, 2024 BCCRT 149
records.jurisdiction: British Columbia Civil Resolution Tribunal
records.notes_on_resolution: Tribunal awarded C$650.88 plus interest and fees.
records.tags: consumer-facing; chatbot; policy-misstatement; negligent-misrepresentation; tribunal; small-claims; public-record-confirmed

records.error_title: ChatGPT fabricated federal cases in an airline personal-injury brief
records.error_description: Counsel filed a brief containing nonexistent cases, false quotations, and false citations generated through ChatGPT. The lawyer then asked ChatGPT whether the cases were real and relied on the chatbot's answer instead of checking legal databases.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Federal reporters, legal research databases, and actual case law
records.mitigation_gap: No independent cite-checking against legal databases; AI self-verification was substituted for source verification; supervising attorney signed without adequate review.
records.reliance_or_harm: Underlying lawsuit was dismissed and counsel/firm were sanctioned.
records.public_matter_name: Mata v. Avianca, Inc., No. 22-cv-1461
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
records.notes_on_resolution: Court imposed a $5,000 sanction and ordered notice to affected judges and client.

records.error_title: ChatGPT-generated appellate filing cited a nonexistent case
records.deployer: Counsel for appellant in Second Circuit appeal
records.error_description: A reply brief cited a case that did not exist. The Second Circuit identified the citation as apparently generated by ChatGPT and referred counsel to the court's grievance panel.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Second Circuit case law and cited legal authority
records.mitigation_gap: No cite-checking caught the nonexistent case before filing; counsel did not ensure AI-assisted legal research matched real authority.
records.reliance_or_harm: Counsel was referred for possible discipline and the court issued a published warning on AI verification duties.
records.public_matter_name: Park v. Kim, No. 22-2057
records.jurisdiction: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit
records.notes_on_resolution: Matter referred to grievance panel; opinion emphasizes duty to verify AI-generated legal citations.

records.error_title: AI-generated cases in Morgan & Morgan opposition brief
records.ai_system_name: ChatGPT / generative AI legal drafting workflow
records.error_description: Attorneys filed briefing containing nonexistent or unsupported case citations attributed to generative AI use. Reporting and case-analysis sources describe a sanctions response after the court identified fabricated authority.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual case law and court record
records.mitigation_gap: AI-generated legal citations were not verified against source materials before filing.
records.reliance_or_harm: Lawyers were fined and the firm faced sanctions-related scrutiny.
records.public_matter_name: Wadsworth v. Walmart Inc.
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Wyoming
records.notes_on_resolution: Included as a notable sanctions event, but the primary court order should be downloaded and archived before treating as fully verified.

records.error_title: AI-drafted FCRA appellate reply brief fabricated quotations
records.ai_system_name: Casetext/CoCounsel and vLex (named; court-found; court did not conclusively pin which product generated hallucinations)
records.error_description: A reply brief contained fabricated quotations and additional misrepresentations. The Fifth Circuit found that AI-generated material had been filed without adequate verification and that counsel's response to the show-cause order compounded the problem.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Fifth Circuit record, quoted opinions, served Rule 11 materials
records.mitigation_gap: No independent verification of quotations or propositions before filing; show-cause response was evasive about AI authorship.
records.reliance_or_harm: Attorney sanctioned and ordered to pay $2,500 to the clerk.
records.public_matter_name: Fletcher v. Experian Information Solutions, Inc., No. 25-20086
records.jurisdiction: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit
records.notes_on_resolution: Fifth Circuit Feb 18, 2026 final order: $2,500 sanction on appellate counsel Heather Hersh under inherent power; district-court counsel Shawn Jaffer Rule 11/$1927 sanctions vacated by separate order. Both Casetext/CoCounsel and vLex named as candidate products; Hersh admitted AI use when asked directly.

records.error_title: AI-generated contract-appeal brief invented cases and record citations
records.error_description: A post-hearing brief contained fictitious cases, false transcript citations, false Rule 4 file citations, and references to transcript pages that did not exist. Counsel admitted AI use in connection with the brief.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: ASBCA precedents, transcript volumes, and Rule 4 appeal file
records.mitigation_gap: Counsel and staff reviewed the brief but did not verify citations against the actual record or authorities; safeguards were found inadequate.
records.reliance_or_harm: Board struck the entire reply brief and denied leave to refile.
records.public_matter_name: Huffman Construction, LLC, ASBCA Nos. 62591, 62783
records.jurisdiction: Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals
records.notes_on_resolution: Reply brief struck; corrected reply denied; board issued broader AI-use caution.

records.error_title: AI-hallucinated class-settlement brief made counsel inadequate
records.ai_system_name: Six unspecified AI tools used concurrently
records.deployer: Plaintiff class counsel in Vuori wage-and-hour case
records.error_description: Class counsel used multiple AI tools to draft and cross-check a class settlement approval brief. The filing contained fabricated quotations from real cases and at least one nonexistent case, and a corrected filing failed to remove the hallucinations.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual case law and settlement approval standards
records.mitigation_gap: AI tools were used to verify each other instead of checking legal databases; corrected filing did not correct false authority.
records.reliance_or_harm: Settlement motions were struck; counsel was found inadequate as class counsel; class settlement collapsed into individual resolution.
records.public_matter_name: Buchanan v. Vuori, Inc., No. 5:23-cv-01121-NC
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
records.notes_on_resolution: Court struck settlement motions with prejudice, imposed a monetary fine, and referred counsel for professional conduct review.

records.error_title: AI-fabricated citations contributed to attorney discipline
records.error_description: Attorney filings contained fabricated AI-generated citations in a federal matter. The AI issue was part of a broader disciplinary record that included evasive responses and other noncompliance.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual case law and federal court filing obligations
records.mitigation_gap: No citation verification; failure to correct when challenged; broader noncompliance with court and bar processes.
records.reliance_or_harm: Attorney was suspended in federal court and later disciplined by the Florida Supreme Court.
records.public_matter_name: In re Thomas Grant Neusom / The Florida Bar v. Neusom
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Florida; Florida Supreme Court
records.notes_on_resolution: AI hallucinations are recorded as corroborating misconduct, not necessarily the sole basis for discipline.

records.error_title: Automated tutor-application screen rejected older applicants
records.error_description: Application software automatically rejected female applicants age 55 or older and male applicants age 60 or older. The EEOC alleged the screening logic unlawfully denied older applicants an employment opportunity.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Age Discrimination in Employment Act
records.mitigation_gap: No pre-deployment age-discrimination audit or human override prevented hard-coded age thresholds from eliminating qualified applicants.
records.reliance_or_harm: More than 200 applicants were allegedly rejected; company settled with EEOC for $365,000 and injunctive relief.
records.public_matter_name: EEOC v. iTutorGroup, Inc., et al., No. 1:22-cv-02565
records.jurisdiction: EEOC; U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York
records.notes_on_resolution: EEOC announced $365,000 settlement and remedial measures.

records.error_title: SafeRent Score allegedly downgraded housing-voucher applicants
records.error_description: Tenant-screening score allegedly relied heavily on credit history and non-tenancy debt in a way that disadvantaged Black and Hispanic rental applicants using housing vouchers. DOJ and HUD filed a statement of interest supporting Fair Housing Act disparate-impact applicability to algorithmic tenant screening.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Fair Housing Act and voucher-backed rent-payment facts
records.mitigation_gap: No adequate fair-housing disparate-impact testing; model allegedly omitted or undervalued voucher payment certainty.
records.reliance_or_harm: Voucher holders allegedly denied housing; settlement created monetary and injunctive relief.
records.public_matter_type: court complaint / agency statement of interest
records.public_matter_name: Louis et al. v. SafeRent Solutions, LLC et al.
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts; DOJ; HUD
records.notes_on_resolution: Settlement announced in 2024; algorithmic tenant screening treated as subject to fair-housing law.

records.error_title: MiDAS automated fraud determinations falsely accused unemployment claimants
records.error_description: MiDAS issued automated unemployment-fraud determinations that were later found overwhelmingly erroneous. Claimants were assessed penalties and collection actions after automated determinations without adequate process.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Unemployment insurance fraud standards and constitutional due-process requirements
records.mitigation_gap: Automated flags lacked meaningful human review and adequate notice/opportunity to contest; error monitoring and escalation failed.
records.reliance_or_harm: Thousands of claimants were wrongfully accused and faced garnishments, penalties, and collection actions; state settled for $20 million.
records.public_matter_name: Bauserman v. Unemployment Insurance Agency
records.jurisdiction: Michigan Court of Claims; Michigan Supreme Court
records.notes_on_resolution: Michigan announced $20 million settlement; Michigan Supreme Court recognized damages availability for the constitutional tort theory.

records.error_title: RUGs care-allocation algorithm cut disabled beneficiaries' Medicaid hours
records.ai_system_name: Resource Utilization Groups (RUGs) computerized care-allocation algorithm
records.error_description: Arkansas replaced nurse assessment logic with a computerized RUGs allocation model that reduced home-care hours for many Medicaid beneficiaries. Litigation alleged the system failed to account for individualized conditions and was implemented without proper rulemaking or due process.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Medicaid home- and community-based services rules, state APA procedures, individualized care needs
records.mitigation_gap: No adequate rulemaking, transparency, individualized validation, or notice process accompanied the algorithmic allocation change.
records.reliance_or_harm: Disabled beneficiaries lost care hours and challenged the system; state-court rulings and later federal litigation led to relief and reforms.
records.public_matter_name: Ledgerwood v. Arkansas Department of Human Services / related federal litigation
records.jurisdiction: Arkansas Supreme Court; U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Arkansas; Eighth Circuit
records.notes_on_resolution: Algorithm invalidated on rulemaking grounds; later proceedings addressed successor systems and systemic relief.
records.tags: internal-use-but-public-consequence; government-benefits; healthcare; eligibility-error; due-process; public-record-confirmed

records.error_title: Rite Aid facial-recognition system generated false shoplifting matches
records.error_description: FTC alleged Rite Aid deployed facial recognition in stores and generated false-positive matches that disproportionately affected certain consumers, including people of color. Employees allegedly acted on false alerts by surveilling, searching, removing, or reporting customers.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Accurate identity evidence and consumer-protection obligations
records.mitigation_gap: Insufficient testing, monitoring, bias controls, employee training, and consumer notice before acting on face-recognition alerts.
records.reliance_or_harm: Consumers were allegedly wrongly accused, embarrassed, or subjected to law-enforcement contact; FTC order banned Rite Aid from using facial recognition for five years.
records.public_matter_name: FTC v. Rite Aid facial-recognition enforcement
records.jurisdiction: U.S. Federal Trade Commission
records.notes_on_resolution: FTC announced five-year facial-recognition ban and algorithmic fairness/monitoring requirements.

records.error_title: Facial-recognition false match led to Robert Williams arrest
records.ai_system_name: Michigan State Police / DataWorks Plus facial-recognition pipeline
records.deployer: Detroit Police Department and Michigan State Police
records.error_description: A grainy surveillance still was run through facial recognition and returned Robert Williams as a candidate. Police allegedly relied on the output and a flawed photo identification process, leading to a wrongful arrest for a theft he did not commit.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity and warrant probable-cause standards
records.mitigation_gap: No policy requiring independent corroboration before arrest; warrant materials did not adequately disclose the limits of the face-recognition lead.
records.reliance_or_harm: Williams was arrested, detained, and later brought a civil-rights suit; settlement included damages and policy reforms.
records.public_matter_name: Williams v. City of Detroit, No. 2:21-cv-10827
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Michigan
records.notes_on_resolution: Settlement included $300,000 and Detroit Police Department facial-recognition reforms.

records.error_title: Facial-recognition lead contributed to Nijeer Parks detention
records.ai_system_name: Clearview AI alleged / out-of-state facial-recognition lead
records.deployer: Woodbridge Police Department and intelligence-center intermediaries
records.error_description: A blurry fake-ID image was run through facial recognition and returned Nijeer Parks as a possible match. Police allegedly obtained a warrant without adequately verifying alibi evidence or disclosing the uncertainty of the technology.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity, alibi evidence, and warrant probable-cause standards
records.mitigation_gap: No adequate alibi check, DNA/fingerprint corroboration, or transparent disclosure of FRT provenance before arrest.
records.reliance_or_harm: Parks spent about 10 days in jail before charges were dismissed; civil-rights litigation followed.
records.public_matter_name: Parks v. McCormac, Civil No. 3:22-cv-05765
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of New Jersey
records.notes_on_resolution: Civil litigation active in reported materials; include as pending.

records.error_title: Facial-recognition-seeded lineup led to Porcha Woodruff arrest
records.error_description: Detroit police used a facial-recognition lead to place Porcha Woodruff in a lineup for a carjacking investigation. The suit alleges obvious exculpatory circumstances, including pregnancy, were not adequately reconciled before arrest.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity and warrant probable-cause standards
records.mitigation_gap: FRT output seeded the lineup without sufficient independent corroboration or review of distinguishing characteristics.
records.reliance_or_harm: Woodruff was arrested while pregnant, detained, and later sued after charges were dropped.
records.public_matter_name: Woodruff v. Oliver, No. 2:23-cv-11597
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Michigan
records.notes_on_resolution: Case reported dismissed at summary judgment in 2025 with appeal planned; keep procedural status under watch.

records.error_title: Retail facial-recognition match allegedly led to Harvey Murphy's arrest
records.deployer: EssilorLuxottica / Macy's loss-prevention personnel and Houston police
records.error_description: A private retail loss-prevention workflow allegedly matched robbery footage to old mugshots of Harvey Murphy and communicated certainty to police. Murphy alleged the match and lineup process caused his arrest despite not being the robber.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity and reliable biometric-comparison standards
records.mitigation_gap: Private FRT comparison lacked qualified-examiner controls; old photographs and employee pre-briefing allegedly contaminated the identification process; police allegedly did not independently investigate.
records.reliance_or_harm: Murphy was detained and alleges severe jailhouse harm; civil case seeks damages.
records.public_matter_name: Murphy v. EssilorLuxottica USA Inc. et al., No. 2024-03265
records.jurisdiction: Harris County, Texas state court; remanded from S.D. Tex.
records.notes_on_resolution: Primary petition available; procedural status should be refreshed from Texas docket before publication.
records.tags: facial-recognition; false-match; wrongful-arrest; consumer-protection; civil-rights; public-record-confirmed

records.error_title: Workday applicant-screening tools allegedly rejected older applicants at scale
records.ai_system_name: Workday HiredScore / Candidate Skills Match tools
records.deployer: Workday, Inc. as HR software vendor
records.error_description: Plaintiff alleges Workday's AI screening tools caused discriminatory job-application rejections based on age, race, and disability-related factors. The case is included as a vendor-liability candidate because the public record identifies AI screening and a court allowed key claims to proceed, but discrete error rows may need to be split by plaintiff or application event.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: ADEA, Title VII, ADA, FEHA, and employer-specific applicant records
records.mitigation_gap: Alleged lack of disparate-impact audit, biased training/preferences, and absence of meaningful human review before rejection.
records.reliance_or_harm: Plaintiff alleges repeated job rejections through Workday systems; ADEA collective certification was reported.
records.public_matter_name: Mobley v. Workday, Inc., No. 3:23-cv-00770
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
records.notes_on_resolution: Included with review flag because the public case is broader than a single bounded error event.

records.error_title: IBM algorithmic rehire screen allegedly blocked applicant due to age
records.ai_system_name: Unnamed IBM algorithmic hiring/rehire screening tool
records.error_description: Plaintiff, a 24-year IBM veteran, alleges an algorithmic hiring/rehire screening tool blocked his rehire at age 48, constituting age discrimination. Filed as Swanson v. International Business Machines Corporation in the Western District of Texas on 2026-05-23. The precise technical nature of the tool (machine-learning vs. rules-based) is not yet established on the public record.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: ADEA and applicant-specific rehire eligibility records
records.mitigation_gap: Alleged absence of meaningful human review of an automated rehire-eligibility screen and no disparate-impact safeguard against age-based exclusion.
records.reliance_or_harm: Plaintiff alleges denial of rehire after 24 years of prior IBM employment, attributed to an automated screen.
records.public_matter_name: Swanson v. International Business Machines Corporation, No. 1:26-cv-01382
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Texas
records.notes_on_resolution: Included by steward at early/filed posture. NOTE: AI system unnamed and ML-vs-rules nature unverified (admission gate 1 partial); complaint not yet pulled from PACER. Verify the tool is genuinely AI and re-confirm on next refresh. Sibling matter: John Loeffler v. IBM, No. 2:25-cv-05765 (C.D. Cal.), same defendant + age/algorithm-hiring theory.
records.tags: internal-use-but-public-consequence; employment; age-discrimination; hiring-screen; algorithmic-denial; needs-complaint-verification

records.error_title: CNN sues Perplexity over alleged use of its content in AI answers
records.ai_system_name: Perplexity answer engine + GenAI products (PerplexityBot / Perplexity-User crawlers; "AI-First" search index; RAG)
records.error_description: CNN (a Warner Bros. Discovery subsidiary) sued Perplexity AI in the Southern District of New York (No. 1:26-cv-04427, filed 2026-05-28; jury demanded). The complaint alleges a two-stage harm: at the input stage, Perplexity’s "PerplexityBot" and "Perplexity-User" crawlers copy CNN content (over 17,000 works) to build an "AI-First" search index; at the output stage, Perplexity’s GenAI products (answer engine, enterprise chatbot, APIs, Comet browser) generate verbatim or substantially similar reproductions. CNN brings Copyright Act (17 U.S.C. § 101 et seq.) and Lanham Act claims, the latter including "confabulated or ‘hallucinated’ content that Perplexity falsely attributes to CNN."
records.canonical_source_conflicted: CNN copyrighted works (17,000+ registrations) and CNN trademarks; 17 U.S.C. § 501 and the Lanham Act
records.mitigation_gap: Alleged crawling/copying of copyrighted content without licensing, and AI output that fabricates and falsely attributes content to CNN without adequate provenance or attribution controls.
records.reliance_or_harm: CNN alleges licensing/market harm from large-scale reproduction plus consumer confusion and brand dilution from hallucinated content falsely attributed to CNN.
records.public_matter_name: Cable News Network, Inc. v. Perplexity AI, Inc., No. 1:26-cv-04427
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
records.notes_on_resolution: Complaint (Doc 1, 54 pp) pulled from RECAP and verified 2026-06-01. Copyright + Lanham (trademark/false-attribution) claims; part of a multi-publisher wave against Perplexity (NYT, Chicago Tribune, Encyclopaedia Britannica/Merriam-Webster, Reddit, Dow Jones).
records.tags: generative-ai; copyright; lanham-act; trademark; content-reproduction; hallucinated-attribution; rag; media

records.error_title: Oklahoma attorney publicly reprimanded for ChatGPT-fabricated citations
records.error_description: In reciprocal attorney discipline, the Oklahoma Supreme Court publicly reprimanded attorney Matthew Brett Reeves (2026 OK 37, decided 2026-05-27) for filing five fabricated citations across two motions in the underlying federal matter Johnson v. Dunn, No. 2:21-cv-1701-AMM (N.D. Ala.). Reeves admitted the citations were "hallucinated by ChatGPT" and that he failed to verify them, violating his firm’s AI policy. The OBA noted this is the first reported Oklahoma attorney-discipline case involving AI-fabricated citations.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law and the duty of candor to the tribunal
records.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated citations against a real reporter before filing.
records.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated authorities submitted to a court; attorney received a public reprimand.
records.public_matter_name: State of Oklahoma ex rel. Oklahoma Bar Association v. Reeves, 2026 OK 37
records.jurisdiction: Supreme Court of Oklahoma
records.notes_on_resolution: Public reprimand (reciprocal; also reprimanded/censured in TX, TN, AL federal court). ChatGPT named and admitted by respondent. Underlying sanction order: Johnson v. Dunn (N.D. Ala.) 2025-07-23.
records.tags: hallucinated-citations; attorney-discipline; chatgpt; reciprocal-discipline; legal-services; sanctions

records.error_title: Pro se appellant sanctioned for DeepSeek-hallucinated case citations
records.error_description: An Ohio trial court sanctioned pro se litigant Xingkui Guo $100 (2025-08-11) for citing five fabricated/mis-cited cases that the court found "appear to be the result of AI-generated hallucinations." On appeal, Guo admitted the mis-citations resulted from his use of the Chinese AI platform "Deepseek." The Sixth District Court of Appeals affirmed on 2026-05-26 (Guo v. Meade Motorcars, L.L.C., 2026-Ohio-1930), rejecting Guo’s motion to set aside the sanction.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine Ohio case law (cases cited did not exist at the given citations)
records.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated authorities before filing.
records.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated citations submitted on appeal; $100 monetary sanction imposed.
records.public_matter_name: Guo v. Meade Motorcars, L.L.C., 2026-Ohio-1930 (No. S-25-035)
records.jurisdiction: Ohio Court of Appeals, Sixth Appellate District
records.notes_on_resolution: $100 sanction imposed by Sandusky County Court of Common Pleas 2025-08-11; Sixth District Court of Appeals affirmed denial of Guo’s motion to set aside, 2026-05-26. DeepSeek named by appellant; court found citations were AI-generated hallucinations.
records.tags: hallucinated-citations; pro-se; deepseek; legal-services; monetary-sanction; appellate-affirmance

records.error_title: Attorney removed and denied fees over Westlaw CoCounsel-fabricated quotations
records.error_description: In a published per curiam opinion, the Sixth Circuit found that attorney Steven N. Howe used Westlaw CoCounsel to draft appellate briefs and filed them without verifying the authorities. The briefs contained three fabricated quotations attributed to real cases and misrepresented the holdings of United States v. Washington and United States v. Anthony. Howe admitted the AI-generated errors.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine holdings of the cited authorities and the duty of candor to the tribunal
records.mitigation_gap: Relied on non-attorney "staff" to supervise AI output; no verification of citations before filing.
records.reliance_or_harm: False quotations and misleading legal arguments submitted in a criminal appeal; judicial resources expended; indigent defendant’s appeal delayed.
records.public_matter_name: United States v. Farris, No. 25-5623 (6th Cir.)
records.jurisdiction: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit
records.notes_on_resolution: Recommended for publication. Howe denied CJA compensation for the appeal, removed as counsel, briefs locked, and referred to the Sixth Circuit Chief Judge (Local Rule 46) and the Kentucky Bar. Notably the fabrications were false quotations from REAL cases, not fictitious cases.

records.error_title: Supervising attorney sanctioned over associate’s AI fake citation (CoCounsel)
records.deployer: Webb Law Group, APC (Lenden Webb, supervising attorney)
records.error_description: A magistrate judge sanctioned supervising attorney Lenden Webb after his firm filed a discovery letter brief containing a nonexistent case (South Pointe Wholesale v. Vilardi) and a misrepresented holding, "caused in part by use of AI." The associate had used Westlaw CoCounsel. Webb was admonished and personally sanctioned for failure to supervise and failure to obey court orders.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; duties of candor and supervision (Cal. Rules of Prof. Conduct 5.1)
records.mitigation_gap: No supervision of associate’s AI use; firm relied on a vendor representation that CoCounsel could not produce hallucinations.
records.reliance_or_harm: Fake citation submitted to the court; judicial resources expended across multiple show-cause orders.
records.public_matter_name: Hill v. Workday, Inc., No. 3:23-cv-06558-PHK
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
records.notes_on_resolution: $1,001 personal sanction + 4 hours CLE + circulation/certification requirements; associate Katherine Cervantes was separately sanctioned (Dkt. 161). Caption shares the name "Workday" with AIEL-2025-018 (Mobley) but is unrelated to the hiring-tool matter — here Workday is merely the opposing party and the AI misconduct is by plaintiff’s counsel.

records.error_title: Plaintiff counsel sanctioned $2,500 for LexisNexis Protégé-hallucinated citations
records.error_description: A district judge sanctioned plaintiff’s counsel Gavril T. Gabriel for citing a fabricated case ("In re Shubert, 799 F.3d 1124") three times across summary-judgment oppositions. Gabriel admitted using LexisNexis+ and its drafting feature Protégé. The court found bad faith, compounded by attempts to bury and minimize the misconduct.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law
records.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated citations; concealment of AI use until ordered to disclose.
records.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated authority cited three times; bad-faith conduct found.
records.public_matter_name: Chaney v. Transdev Services Inc., No. 2:24-cv-10761-ODW (AJRx)
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Central District of California
records.notes_on_resolution: $2,500 total ($500 + $1,000 + $1,000) for bad faith; ordered to file a disclosure declaration in every Central District of California case in which he appears.

records.error_title: Attorney sanctioned $2,500; court found citations from "some kind of AI program"
records.ai_system_name: Unspecified generative-AI program (court finding; attorney’s LexisNexis attribution not credited)
records.error_description: A magistrate judge sanctioned attorney Tricia S. Lindsay after she filed two opposition memoranda citing numerous nonexistent cases. By process of elimination (no human drafter claimed), the court found the citations "were generated as a result of the use of some kind of AI program," expressly declining to credit Lindsay’s belated suggestion that LexisNexis was the source. The court found subjective bad faith.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; Rule 11 duty of reasonable inquiry
records.mitigation_gap: No citation verification; obfuscation about the source of the fabricated citations.
records.reliance_or_harm: Numerous fabricated citations across multiple filings; referred (with co-counsel) to the Second Circuit Grievance Panel.
records.public_matter_name: Jimenez-Fogarty v. Fogarty, No. 1:24-cv-08705-JLR-GWG
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
records.notes_on_resolution: $2,500 Rule 11 sanction; ordered to notify the judge in every pending case where she appears; separately referred (with co-counsel Jo Saint-George) to the Second Circuit Grievance Panel per Ioio v. City of New York (2d Cir. Mar. 16, 2026). NOTE: the Charlotin "LexisNexis" tool tag is NOT supported — the court declined to credit LexisNexis as the source.

records.error_title: Alabama Supreme Court sanctions attorney for AI-fabricated authorities
records.ai_system_name: Unnamed generative-AI research tool (court finding; counsel conceded)
records.error_description: The Supreme Court of Alabama found that briefs filed by appellants’ counsel ("Hall") contained numerous invalid citations the court determined were "AI hallucinations," i.e., fake authorities created by an AI system. Counsel conceded the improper AI use, describing it as his first use of an AI research tool. The court dismissed the appeal as grossly deficient and imposed sanctions.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine legal authorities; Alabama Rules of Appellate Procedure
records.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated authorities before filing.
records.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated authorities forced opposing party and the court to expend resources; appeal dismissed.
records.public_matter_name: Ibach v. Stewart (Ala.)
records.jurisdiction: Supreme Court of Alabama
records.notes_on_resolution: Counsel ordered to pay $17,200 in attorney fees and costs to the appellee, plus double costs, and barred from further filings absent leave; appeal dismissed.

records.error_title: Attorney sanctioned $5,000 for repeated improper AI use (second offense)
records.ai_system_name: Unnamed generative AI (ChatGPT referenced in order)
records.error_description: A district judge sanctioned defendants’ attorney Raja Rajan $5,000 under Rule 11(c) for his repeated improper use of generative AI in filings — his second such violation. The court rejected his plea for a lighter penalty, noting prior sanctions exceeding $73,000.

Roots' paralegal admitted in a sworn declaration that earlier drafts included "AI-assisted research features" from Lexis tools. Roots himself pledged to no longer rely on AI-assisted research summaries. The court imposed a $1,500 Rule 11 sanction and dismissed the case for lack of standing.
records.canonical_source_conflicted: Non-existent or misrepresented federal civil rights and animal-law authorities; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
records.mitigation_gap: Counsel used AI-assisted research tools without adequate verification; admitted through pledged remedial measures and paralegal declaration.
records.reliance_or_harm: $1,500 Rule 11 sanction; case dismissed for lack of Article III standing.
records.public_matter_name: Maldonado v. Professional Animal Retirement Center (PARC), No. 1:25-cv-00454-HAB-ALT (N.D. Ind.)
records.jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Indiana
records.notes_on_resolution: Charlotin tracker date (Feb 27) is the Show Cause Order; actual sanctions order is April 1, 2026. Case involves Joseph Maldonado-Passage (Joe Exotic), federally incarcerated.
records.tags: hallucinated-citations; attorney-discipline; admitted-ai; unnamed-ai; lexis-ai-features; dismissed; legal-services

records.candidate_title: Pro se litigant sanctioned $1,000 for fabricated case citations
records.candidate_matter: William C. Maxwell v. Dakota Michael, Officer, et al., No. 1:24-cv-01365-JRS-TAB (S.D. Ind.)
records.reason_for_review: Gate 1 (AI) NOT established. Verified order (Doc 206, filed 2026-05-26, Mag. J. Baker) imposes a $1,000 Rule 11 sanction + $886.80 deposition costs for fabricating citations across 37+ instances, but the court EXPRESSLY declined to find AI use: "Whether or not Plaintiff used AI, his conduct remains sanctionable." Plaintiff claimed he inserted fake-citation "placeholders"; court only noted the fabrications bear "hallmarks of generative AI hallucinations." No named AI system. Charlotin caption was wrong (defendant is "Dakota Michael, Officer, et al.," not "Chelsey Smith"); date-conflict resolved (March 2026 = underlying R&R adoption; sanction order = 2026-05-26).
records.next_verification_step: Steward decision: does "hallmarks of AI" without a court finding satisfy admission gate 1, or exclude as non-AI fabrication? No further docket work needed — order is final on sanction.

records.reason_for_review: Gate 1 FAIL (2026-06-02 re-verification). Court language is hedged throughout: 'note the possibility', 'apparent unchecked use', 'apparent reliance on AI'. No actor admission (pro se, no disclosure). Court expressly declined to sanction. Briefs struck solely for Rule 341 noncompliance. Centient AI appears in plaintiffs' own briefs as a hyperlink reference — circumstantially strong but not an admission and not adopted as a court finding. Cannot promote to included under strict gate-1 standard. Retain for future promotion if gate-1 standard relaxes to include strong-inference court language, or if plaintiffs' briefs are obtained as a corroborating source.
records.next_verification_step: Pull plaintiffs' original briefs to confirm Centient AI product reference is unambiguous; then decide whether to relax gate-1 to cover strong-inference AI findings for pro se litigants.
